perturbation.original_text: Erchonia Warranty and Warranty Limitations: Other than the written warranty accompanying the Products, Erchonia DISCLAIMS ALL OTHER REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, INCLUDING WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, OR NON-INFRINGEMENT OF THIRD PARTY INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS.
perturbation.changed_text: Erchonia Warranty and Warranty Limitations: Erchonia DISCLAIMS ALL REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'Other than the written warranty accompanying the Products, INCLUDING WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, OR NON-INFRINGEMENT OF THIRD PARTY INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS', the contract attempts to remove implied warranties, which may not be permissible under California law, especially the implied warranty of merchantability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code § 2314 - Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2314 establishes the implied warranty of merchantability, which guarantees that goods are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. By broadly disclaiming all implied warranties without explicitly acknowledging or providing the protections of merchantability, the modified text contradicts California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted b
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the phrase explicitly disclaiming warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, and non-infringement. According to the perturbation explanation, this attempts to remove implied warranties, which is questionable under California law, especially the implied warranty of merchantability. The law explanation states that California Commercial Code Section 2314 establishes the implied warranty of merchantability, ensuring goods are fit for their ordinary purposes. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing COM 2314, confirms that unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty of merchantability is implied in a contract for the sale of goods if the seller is a merchant of those goods. The omission of the original text arguably contradicts this by attempting to disclaim all implied warranties without proper exclusion, which could fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall promptly notify Erchonia of any complaint about negative, unwanted, deleterious, or other side effects due to the use of the Products, including the complainant's name, contact information, and date of complaint.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall notify Erchonia of any major complaint about significant side effects due to the use of the Products.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement to provide the complainant's name, contact information, and date of complaint, and also adds an ambiguous qualifier ('major' and 'significant'). This violates the spirit and potentially the letter of adverse event reporting requirements, which often mandate complete and detailed information for proper investigation, especially with medical devices. This omission introduces legal risk as it hinders Erchonia's ability to fulfill its regulatory obligations for reporting adverse events to the FDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Part 803 outlines the Medical Device Reporting (MDR) requirements for manufacturers, importers, and user facilities. By omitting the requirement to collect the complainant's name, contact information, and date of complaint, the modified text prevents the collection of essential information required for proper adverse event reporting to the FDA. This hinders the ability to investigate and report adverse events thoroughly, violating FDA regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text omits essential information required for adverse event reporting, as outlined in 21 CFR Part 803 (Medical Device Reporting). By removing the requirement to collect the complainant's name, contact information, and date of complaint, the modified text directly hinders the ability to thoroughly investigate and report adverse events to the FDA, creating a legal contradiction. The Omission Legal Contradiction definition is triggered because legally mandated information is removed, making the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: Payments received after the 30 day calendar period will be subject to a late fee of 1.5% of the invoice amount.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments received after the 30 day calendar period may be subject to a late fee.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces a definite late fee with a discretionary one. While seemingly minor, it removes the consumer's (Distributor's) certainty regarding late payment penalties. This can lead to disputes and potential violations of California law if the 'may be subject to' clause is used arbitrarily or in a way that is deemed unconscionable. Deleting the precise percentage exposes the distributor to unspecified and possibly predatory fees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 1671 - Validity; Standards for Determination; Applicability
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1671 governs liquidated damages clauses, including late fees. While it allows for liquidated damages, it requires that they be reasonable and proportionate to the actual damages suffered. By making the late fee discretionary ('may be subject to') and removing the specified amount, the modified text creates the potential for unreasonable or unconscionable late fees, violating the spirit and potentially the letter of California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4.5. LIQUIDATED DAMAGES [1671 - 1681] ( Heading of Title 4.5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1671- 1671.] ( Heading of Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) 1671. (a) This section does not apply in any case where another statute expressly applicable to the contract prescribes the rules or standard for determining the validity of a provision in the contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract. (b) Except as provided in subdivision (c), a provision in a contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract is valid unless the party seeking to invalidate the provision establishes that the provision was unreasonable under the circumstances existing at the time the contract was made. (c) The validity of a liquidated damages provision shall be determined under subdivision (d) and not under subdivision (b) where the liquidated damages are sought to be recovered from either: (1) A party to a contract for the retail purchase, or rental, by such party of personal property or services, primarily for the party’s personal, family, or househ
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a definite late fee (1.5% of the invoice amount) with a discretionary one ('may be subject to'). This omission creates a potential violation of California Civil Code Section 1671, which governs liquidated damages clauses, including late fees. While Section 1671(b) states that a liquidated damages provision is valid unless proven unreasonable, the removal of a specific amount and the introduction of discretion ('may be subject to') can lead to unreasonable or unconscionable late fees. The original text provided certainty, while the changed text opens the door to arbitrary and potentially excessive fees, contradicting the intention of the law to ensure reasonableness in liquidated damages.
